.codex.purpose: Tooling-only root configuring how this repo runs under OpenAI Codex. Splits into agents/ (Codex agent profiles, e.g. the archie_analysis subagent) and rules/ (execpolicy auto-approve rules for Archie's deep-scan / intent-layer / share / viewer scripts). Contains no application or build logic — both children are generated by the Archie Codex installer.

patterns.name: Agent profiles vs execpolicy rules separation
patterns.description: agents/ holds one TOML per agent profile (workspace-write sandbox, scoped writes); rules/ holds prefix_rule entries that auto-approve the exact shell commands Archie workflows invoke. The two concerns never mix.
patterns.example: // .codex/agents/archie-analysis.toml configures the agent; .codex/rules/archie.rules auto-approves its commands

patterns.name: Narrow per-script auto-approve
patterns.description: Each Archie workflow script gets its own prefix_rule with an explicit match list rather than a blanket python3 allow; most-restrictive-wins precedence governs overlaps.
patterns.example: prefix_rule { program = "python3", args = [...] } // one per .archie script

key_file_guides.role: Codex subagent profile used by Archie scan/deep-scan/intent-layer workflows.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Do not widen sandbox_mode beyond workspace-write or remove the 'stay within your assigned task / do not paste large artifacts' developer_instructions.

key_file_guides.role: Generated execpolicy rules auto-approving Archie workflow commands.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Regenerated at install time — hand edits do not persist; a new .archie workflow script without a matching prefix_rule will prompt the user mid-run.

anti_patterns: Adding application or build logic here — this folder only configures Codex agents and execpolicy.
Widening sandbox_mode beyond workspace-write without intent — undermines the scoped-write safety guarantee.
Granting broad allow rules (e.g. all python3) instead of script-scoped prefix_rules with explicit match lists.
Hand-editing archie.rules and expecting it to persist — it is regenerated by the installer.

decisions.decision: Archie ships a dedicated Codex worker agent rather than reusing the default agent.
decisions.rationale: Bounds automated Archie runs with scoped writes and explicit instructions, keeping them safe and predictable.

app.purpose: Organizational root for application assembly, split into app/common (Google Wire DI provider sets that assemble every domain service into binary-specific run graphs) and app/config (the single shared Viper config.Configuration struct used by all seven binaries). Contains zero business logic; nothing in openmeter/* or pkg/* may import either child.

patterns.name: Leaf-node import direction
patterns.description: app/common depends on all openmeter/* and pkg/* packages; the reverse is forbidden and causes import cycles. app/config is imported only by app/common and cmd/*.
patterns.example: // OK: app/common/billing.go imports openmeter/billing — NEVER: openmeter/billing imports app/common

patterns.name: One child per concern
patterns.description: app/common owns runtime wiring (provider sets, hook/validator registration, noop fallbacks); app/config owns pure data structs with Validate() and SetViperDefaults. The two concerns must not bleed together.
patterns.example: // app/config/billing.go: type BillingConfig struct{...}; func (c BillingConfig) Validate() error

patterns.name: Credits guard at every ledger-touching provider
patterns.description: Any app/common provider wiring ledger-backed features must independently check creditsConfig.Enabled and return a noop when false; a single centralized guard is insufficient because credits cross-cut many call graphs.

patterns.name: Registry structs for multi-service domains
patterns.description: Cohesive services group into typed registry structs (BillingRegistry, AppRegistry, ChargesRegistry) with nil-safe accessors (ChargesServiceOrNil()) instead of individual injection into router.Config.
patterns.example: svc := registry.ChargesServiceOrNil(); if svc == nil { return nil /* credits disabled */ }

patterns.name: Hook/validator registration as provider side-effects
patterns.description: Cross-domain hooks (billing→customer, ledger→customer) and request validators register inside app/common provider functions as side-effects to avoid circular imports between domain packages.

key_file_guides.role: Wire provider set for billing; constructs billing.Service and BillingRegistry including charges adapters.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Adding business logic in providers; accessing BillingRegistry.Charges directly instead of ChargesServiceOrNil().

key_file_guides.role: Wires ledger services; returns noop implementations when credits.enabled=false at every provider independently.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Any new ledger-touching provider lacking a creditsConfig.Enabled guard returning a noop type.

key_file_guides.role: Registers customer service hooks and request validators from billing/ledger as Wire provider side-effects.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Calling RegisterHooks/RegisterRequestValidator from domain packages instead of here — causes circular imports.

key_file_guides.role: Registers charge-type line engines via billing.Service.RegisterLineEngine() as provider side-effects.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Calling RegisterLineEngine from domain packages or cmd/* — must happen here to avoid circular imports.

key_file_guides.role: Defines the BillingWorker Wire set composing the provider sets cmd/billing-worker needs.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Missing hook providers register silently — Wire sees only types, not side-effects; omitting one drops the hook with no compile error.

key_file_guides.role: Root config.Configuration struct for all seven binaries; SetViperDefaults is the single Viper registration point.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Adding config fields without a matching Validate() check and Configure* default registration.

anti_patterns: Importing app/common from any openmeter/* or pkg/* package — reverses the leaf-node import direction and creates cycles.
Adding business logic (validation, computation, state mutation, panic/os.Exit) inside provider functions — providers may only construct and wire.
Creating a ledger-backed provider without a creditsConfig.Enabled guard returning a noop.
Calling RegisterHooks/RegisterRequestValidator from domain constructors instead of app/common providers.
Accessing BillingRegistry.Charges directly without ChargesServiceOrNil() — panics when credits are disabled.

decisions.decision: All DI wiring concentrated in app/common with plain constructors in domain packages.
decisions.rationale: Wire produces compile-time-verified dependency graphs; a single edit point adds a provider to any binary without duplicating constructor chains per cmd/*.

decisions.decision: Credits guarded independently in each ledger-touching provider rather than one choke point.
decisions.rationale: Credits cross-cut HTTP handlers, customer hooks, namespace provisioning, and charge creation — no single injection point dominates all paths reaching ledger writes.

decisions.decision: Registry structs instead of individual service injection into router.Config.
decisions.rationale: Nil-safe accessors (ChargesServiceOrNil) encapsulate the credits-disabled nil case; individual field access would scatter nil checks across callers.

code_examples.scenario: Adding a new ledger-backed provider that respects the credits flag

docs.purpose: Documentation-only root split into ADRs (docs/decisions/) recording immutable architectural choices and operator-facing migration guides (docs/migration-guides/) for breaking changes between versions, plus standalone developer/operator guides (database-migration, event-ingestion, seeder, stripe-dev). Contains no Go source; changes here affect operator understanding and historical record, not compile-time behavior.

patterns.name: ADR immutability + supersession
patterns.description: Existing ADRs in docs/decisions/ record a past decision and must never be edited to change it; create a new sequentially numbered ADR to supersede.
patterns.example: // 0005-clickhouse.md documents the ksqlDB→ClickHouse switch; a reversal needs 0006-*.md, never an edit to 0005

patterns.name: One migration guide per breaking change
patterns.description: Each file in docs/migration-guides/ covers exactly one breaking change, with date-prefixed filename, Before/After API examples, and a deprecation timeline table.

patterns.name: Audit queries before destructive SQL
patterns.description: Migration guides that include SQL show a SELECT audit query before any UPDATE/DELETE so operators can verify scope before executing.
patterns.example: -- Audit first: SELECT id, key FROM subjects WHERE customer_id IS NULL; -- Then migrate: UPDATE subjects SET ...

key_file_guides.role: ADR: choice of Kafka as the event streaming platform.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Do not edit to reflect a different decision; create a new 000N-*.md ADR to supersede.

key_file_guides.role: ADR: ClickHouse replaces ksqlDB for analytics — authoritative source for why ClickHouse is the analytics store.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Any ClickHouse architectural change requires a new superseding ADR, not an edit here.

key_file_guides.role: Operator guide covering autoMigrate modes (ent, migration, false) and the Atlas workflow.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Keep in sync with actual atlas.hcl and Makefile targets when migration commands change.

key_file_guides.role: User-facing explanation of CloudEvents format, JSONPath meter configuration, and the 32-day deduplication window.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Any change to ingest API contract or deduplication window must be reflected here.

key_file_guides.role: Developer guide for working against a Stripe test account including webhook setup and psql commands.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Keep webhook URL patterns in sync with actual API routes; update psql column names if the Ent schema changes.

anti_patterns: Editing existing ADR files to change the recorded decision — always create a new sequentially numbered ADR.
Combining multiple unrelated breaking changes in a single migration guide — one breaking change per file.
Writing a migration guide without audit SELECT queries before destructive UPDATE/DELETE statements.
Using docs/decisions for operational runbooks or migration SQL — those belong in docs/migration-guides.
Omitting the 'Considered Options' and 'Cons' sections when writing a new ADR — every decision must document its tradeoffs.

decisions.decision: ADRs in docs/decisions/ are append-only historical records.
decisions.rationale: Immutable records prevent revisionist history and give an auditable trail of why the current architecture exists.

decisions.decision: SQL migration scripts embedded directly in migration guides rather than separate .sql files.
decisions.rationale: Self-contained guides are easier for operators to follow without cross-referencing, and inline context explains each statement.
